key_insights.insight: Kepler's discovery process resembled a high-temperature LLM generating many ideas, needing verification against precise data.
key_insights.quote: Kepler was a high-temperature LLM, where Newton comes up with this explanation of why the three laws of planetary motion must be true.

key_insights.insight: The bottleneck in science has shifted from idea generation to verification due to AI lowering the cost of generating theories.
key_insights.quote: Now the bottleneck is different. So we're now in a situation where suddenly people can generate thousands of theories for a given scientific problem. And now we have to verify them, evaluate them.

key_insights.insight: AI currently solves low-hanging fruit math problems (those with little prior literature) but struggles with deeper problems requiring new techniques; success per attempt is low but scaled attempts yield occasional wins.
key_insights.quote: So there will be a similar thing happening with, you know, there are hundreds of really prestigious, difficult math problems out there. A couple may, you know, some A.m. may get lucky and actually solve them.

key_insights.insight: AI excels at breadth (generating many ideas) while humans excel at depth; science should be redesigned to leverage AI for broad exploration and humans for deep work.
key_insights.quote: So as I said, we do it. we should have a lot more effort in creating very broad classes of problems to work on rather than one or two really deep important problems.

key_insights.insight: Formal proof assistants like Lean allow decomposition of proofs into atomic steps, enabling assessment of which lemmas are key and facilitating collaborative refinement.
key_insights.quote: The beauty of formalizing a proof in something like Lean is that you can take any piece of it and study it atomically.

key_insights.insight: Scientific progress often comes from deleting incorrect assumptions (e.g., Aristotelian notion of rest) rather than merely adding new theories.
key_insights.quote: often progress has been made actually not by adding more theories, but by deleting some assumptions that you have in your mind.

topics: Breadth vs depth in scientific research

actionable_advice.advice: Use AI to generate broad problem sets for initial exploration, then have human experts focus on the difficult subproblems identified.
actionable_advice.how_to: Create AI-driven problem banks that output many candidate problems; researchers review outputs to select promising hard problems for deep work.

actionable_advice.advice: Employ formal proof assistants (Lean) with rubrics to evaluate AI-generated proofs on tool use, alternative paths, and clarity, enabling iterative refinement.
actionable_advice.how_to: Train or prompt AI to produce Lean code and then apply rubrics assessing steps like reaching for right tools, checking work, exploring alternatives.

actionable_advice.advice: Develop standardized challenge datasets for AI scientific hypothesis generation that require reporting both successes and failures to avoid publication bias.
actionable_advice.how_to: Curate a set of open problems with known difficulty levels; mandate that AI evaluations publish success rates and failure analyses.

actionable_advice.advice: Leverage AI to automatically apply known techniques from literature to new problems before attempting novel approaches, increasing efficiency.
actionable_advice.how_to: Prompt AI to retrieve and try all relevant standard methods on a given problem, documenting which succeed or fail, to guide human insight.

contrarian_claims.claim: Idea generation is no longer the bottleneck in science; verification and evaluation have become the limiting factor.
contrarian_claims.context: Traditionally, generating hypotheses was seen as the hard, creative part; AI now produces ideas cheaply, shifting the challenge to filtering and validating them.

contrarian_claims.claim: A low success rate (1-2%) per AI attempt can still yield valuable scientific progress when scaled massively, contrary to the expectation that each AI-generated hypothesis must be high-quality.
contrarian_claims.context: Many dismiss AI's low per-trial success as useless, but large-scale trials can produce occasional breakthroughs akin to Kepler's lucky fit with few data points.

contrarian_claims.claim: Deleting entrenched assumptions (e.g., Aristotelian rest) can drive progress more than adding new theories, challenging the view that science advances mainly by accumulating theories.
contrarian_claims.context: Historical examples show progress often required discarding false intuitions rather than just adding complexity.
